title: Terraform Core Workflow
subtitle: Practical lifecycle: write, check, plan, review, apply, and manage change safely

nodes.label: Write Configuration
nodes.definition: Terraform work starts by writing configuration files that describe the desired infrastructure.
nodes.whyItMatters: The files are the source of truth for what Terraform should manage.
nodes.safeSentence: The workflow starts by describing the desired infrastructure in Terraform configuration files.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not think the files are just commands. They describe the desired end state.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Terraform begins with configuration files that describe the infrastructure the team wants to create or manage.

nodes.label: Initialize
nodes.definition: Initialize prepares the working directory by setting up the backend and downloading needed providers and modules.
nodes.whyItMatters: Terraform cannot plan or apply correctly until the working directory is initialized.
nodes.safeSentence: Terraform init prepares the project by downloading providers and setting up the backend.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not skip init after adding or changing providers, modules, or backend configuration.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I think of terraform init as the setup step that prepares the workspace before planning or applying changes.

note.summary:
Usual command: terraform init

This is often the first command in a project. It prepares the directory so Terraform knows which providers and modules are required.

nodes.label: Format
nodes.definition: Format rewrites Terraform configuration into the standard style.
nodes.whyItMatters: Consistent formatting makes Terraform easier to read, review, and maintain.
nodes.safeSentence: Terraform fmt is a hygiene step that keeps configuration readable and consistent.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not treat formatting as logic validation. It improves style, not correctness.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I use terraform fmt as a simple consistency step before review, similar to formatting code before a pull request.

note.summary:
Usual command: terraform fmt

Formatting is not correctness, but it reduces review noise and improves collaboration.

nodes.label: Validate
nodes.definition: Validate checks whether the Terraform configuration is syntactically valid and internally consistent.
nodes.whyItMatters: It catches configuration problems before planning or applying changes.
nodes.safeSentence: Terraform validate catches configuration issues before planning or applying.
nodes.commonTrap: Validate does not prove the cloud change is safe. It mainly checks the configuration structure.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Validate is an early quality check. It helps catch syntax and configuration issues before moving to plan.

note.summary:
Usual command: terraform validate

Think of this as a structure check. It can catch syntax mistakes and missing required arguments, but plan is still needed for real change preview.

nodes.label: Plan
nodes.definition: Plan previews what Terraform intends to create, change, or destroy.
nodes.whyItMatters: Plan is the main safety checkpoint before touching real infrastructure.
nodes.safeSentence: Terraform plan is the preview step before changing real infrastructure.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not treat plan output as noise. It is the evidence for what will change.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Plan is critical because it shows the blast radius before apply.

note.summary:
Usual command: terraform plan

Plan compares configuration, state, and provider data to propose changes.
Ask: what is created, changed, or destroyed, and is that blast radius acceptable?

image.caption: Core Terraform workflow from .tf files through apply, outputs/state, and destroy caution.

nodes.label: Review Plan
nodes.definition: Review Plan means a human or team checks the planned changes before applying.
nodes.whyItMatters: Terraform can create, modify, or destroy important infrastructure, so the plan needs human judgment.
nodes.safeSentence: The plan should be reviewed carefully before apply, especially in shared or production environments.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not blindly approve a plan just because Terraform generated it.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I see plan review as the change-control checkpoint in Terraform.

note.summary:
This is where Terraform becomes a team workflow. In mature environments, review happens via PR or CI gates.

Safe framing: I understand plan review is essential because it shows what will actually change before execution.

nodes.label: Apply
nodes.definition: Apply performs the approved Terraform changes against the target platform.
nodes.whyItMatters: This is where the reviewed plan becomes real infrastructure.
nodes.safeSentence: Terraform apply should happen only after the plan has been reviewed.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not casually run apply in the wrong workspace, account, or environment.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Apply is the execution step where Terraform carries out the reviewed infrastructure changes.

note.summary:
Usual command: terraform apply

Apply calls platform APIs through providers and makes real changes. Safe habit: always read and confirm the plan before approval.

nodes.label: Outputs
nodes.definition: Outputs expose useful values after Terraform creates or reads infrastructure.
nodes.whyItMatters: Outputs help people and downstream automation find important resulting values.
nodes.safeSentence: Outputs make important infrastructure values visible after Terraform runs.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not expose secrets casually through outputs.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Outputs are useful for sharing values like endpoint names, IDs, URLs, or other created resource details.

nodes.label: Change Over Time
nodes.definition: Terraform is used repeatedly as infrastructure evolves, not only for the first creation.
nodes.whyItMatters: Real infrastructure changes over time, and Terraform helps manage those changes in a controlled way.
nodes.safeSentence: Terraform is not just for initial creation; it manages infrastructure changes over time.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not manually change Terraform-managed resources and forget to reflect the change in code.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Terraform supports ongoing infrastructure management by repeating the plan/review/apply cycle as requirements change.

note.summary:
Typical changes include tags, sizing, networking, permissions, and monitoring.
Safe pattern: update code first, then plan, review, and apply to avoid drift.

nodes.label: Destroy Carefully
nodes.definition: Destroy removes Terraform-managed infrastructure and must be treated as risky.
nodes.whyItMatters: Destroy can delete real resources and data if used carelessly.
nodes.safeSentence: Terraform destroy is powerful and should be used only with clear intent and review.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not run destroy in the wrong environment or against long-lived production resources.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Destroy should be handled carefully because it can remove infrastructure that systems depend on.

note.summary:
Commands: terraform destroy or terraform plan -destroy

Useful for temporary labs, high risk for shared/production environments. Always verify workspace, account, and plan before approval.

nodes.label: Terraform 1000-Foot View
nodes.definition: Open the previous map that explains Terraform's basic mental model.
nodes.whyItMatters: This gives Sean a doorway back to the big-picture Terraform map.
nodes.safeSentence: Before going deeper into workflow details, it helps to keep the 1000-foot Terraform model in mind.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not mix this navigation bubble with the workflow sequence. It is a map doorway, not a workflow step.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Terraform's workflow makes more sense when connected back to desired state, providers, resources, plan, apply, state, drift, and team safety.

paths.label: Terraform Core Workflow Walk
paths.description: Walk through the practical Terraform lifecycle from authoring through safe execution and lifecycle risk.
paths.rehearsalPrompt: Explain the Terraform lifecycle and where safety checks happen before and after apply.
